items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What is the difference between a food allergy and an intolerance?
items.correct: An allergy involves the immune system; intolerance does not.
items.misconception: They are the same with different severity.
items.random_wrong: Allergies are temporary, intolerances are permanent.
items.why: Students treat these as severity points on one spectrum. They have different mechanisms - allergies trigger immune (IgE) responses (potentially fatal anaphylaxis); intolerances are digestive (e.g., lactase deficiency).

items.q: Does 'organic' mean absolutely no pesticides were ever used?
items.correct: Not quite - organic farming avoids artificial (synthetic) pesticides and fertilisers, but a restricted list of naturally derived treatments is permitted.
items.misconception: Yes - organic crops are never treated with anything at all.
items.random_wrong: Organic means the food was grown indoors.
items.why: Students take 'organic' as 'zero intervention'. Certification bodies (e.g. the Soil Association) ban synthetic pesticides and artificial fertilisers but allow certain natural products and methods. The focus is on soil health and biodiversity, not literally spraying nothing.

items.q: Is locally grown food automatically the most sustainable choice?
items.correct: Not always - production methods count too; energy-hungry local production can beat imported field-grown food on emissions.
items.misconception: Yes - 'local' always guarantees the lowest environmental impact.
items.random_wrong: Sustainability only depends on the packaging colour.
items.why: Food miles are only one slice of the footprint. Out-of-season local crops may need heated greenhouses or long refrigerated storage, costing more energy than importing produce grown in natural sunshine. Judge season + method + transport together.
The answer examiners want is: Distance food travels. “Calories per gram” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Artificial pesticides and fertilisers. “All vegetables” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many trust labels are introduced in this foundation?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
